title: UC SCHOOL STEREOTYPES EXPLAINED | UCLA, UCB, UCR, UCI etc.
body:
"UCSD students are UCLA rejects"

Ahah :(
I'm going to UCSD because I was rejected to UCLA

Update: YOOOOO I JUST GOT A NOTIFICATION THAT SOMEONE REPLIED TO THIS. I HAD TOTALLY FORGOTTEN ABOUT THIS COMMENT... IT HAS NOW BEEN 2 YRS WTF

I am actually super happy at UCSD and am grateful that I came here. I have great friends, I am in a project team, have had my fair share of partying. PRE COVID, I used to see the most beautiful sunsets as I walked back to my dorm from class.

I now laugh at my own saltiness and sadness from 2 years ago. I came to UCSD for an engineering degree and it's not easy at all, but it's cool that I'm in a top 10 Engineering school...

Just know that you'll find your place and even though it's cliche AS HELL, a school doesn't define you.
author: @princesscookies7537

title: UC SCHOOL STEREOTYPES EXPLAINED | UCLA, UCB, UCR, UCI etc.
body:
I'm attending UCR this fall and I want to defeat the stereotype that all students there are UC Rejects. I was accepted to UCLA, UCSD, and UCI and actually preferred UCR because of the location, the financial aid, the size of the campus, and UCR just felt like home. The other schools were just not ideal and I'm glad I focused on what I was most comfortable with rather than prestige.

Update: I graduated this past March from UCR with a bachelors in psychology, completely debt free. I made great connections and wonderful friends through student orgs and took advantage of the many opportunities that UCR had to offer. I truly had an amazing time at UCR. It’s sad to see that my time there is over now but I’ll always be grateful for the experience.
author: @marywilder84

title: Community College vs University | Brett Cooper
body: I went back to Southwestern junior college in San Diego just to take a few business courses so I can get a staff office job. I did so well that I ended up as a double major in Business Administration/Management and Psychology. I transferred to San Diego State University (SDSU) with a 3.50+ GPA. I was accepted to the SDSU business department after a bunch of strict requirements and a difficult upper-division entrance exam. It was considered one of the top 10 business programs in the U.S. back in the mid-1990s and thankfully I ended up with a business degree. Junior colleges are one the best thing that happened to me…especially for a late bloomer like myself.
author: @KennethW007

title: MY COLLEGE EXPERIENCE (ucla film school)
body:
"College has become really all about the 'college experience', rather than the college education, which is what you're here to get."
YES YES YES.
I'm a freshman in college, and I chose to stay home and go to community college, and my junior/senior year of HS when everybody's figuring out where theyre gonna go, my heart BROKE at the thought of community college. I had been WAITING for that "fun college experience": partying, meeting hot guys, a dorm, and freedom to start fresh, but the truth is, thats all SO overrated. Half of my old HS friends who went away end up alone in their dorm every night, wishing they decided to save money going to CC. My point it, any high schoolers reading this: don't be too attached to the idea of that "college experience." Go where you'll get the best education without selling your life away. College is ridiculously expensive, especially when the first year or two are just gen-eds that you can take anywhere and have nothing to do with your major. Stay focused on what you NEED and what you WANT will follow.
author: @zoeh.2361

title: MY COLLEGE EXPERIENCE (ucla film school)
body: I feel like you're Hermione Granger, being able to be at multiple places at once. You were able to double up on classes, work, do internships, and film youtube videos within 3 years of school??? :O girl you're superwoman lolol
author: @AM-br8bg

title: MY COLLEGE EXPERIENCE (ucla film school)
body: College is where I saw so many people with depression, anxiety and just terrible mental health. Students joke all the time, myself included, that so and so class is killing them but it's high key reality. I've spent nights with friends crying over not knowing what's our purpose is and what's the point of a degree. My friends that have graduated already felt more anxiety for joining the workforce than actually wanting to celebrate. I have 2 more semesters before I graduate and I was fortunate enough to realize I needed a mental health break and took a semester off. In that time, I figured out my plans for my life. Sadly, i think it's such a bad stigma in the US to not finish in 4 yrs and people don't slow down to really analyze their future. If you want to go to college or are in college now, please take the time to think about how you've grown as a person academic wise, socially and how you want to see yourself in 10 years.
author: @Themoment888

author: @professionalprocrastinator

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body:
My soul was getting crushed just watching this.

I do believe the scarcity and competitive environment is intentionally created. A university as rich and esteemed as that has no reason to be subjecting students to ANY of this. At. All.

They really can't hire more professors to accommodate for the students? Why accept more students if there isn't enough staff for it. It's a total scam. It's about money.

I've never heard of grade deflation before, but that is just plain wrong.
author: @sweetsunshine777

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body: The best advice someone ever gave me was to do my first two years at a nice community college and then transfer to a large university. It Gives u a chance to get some easy As plus u save tens of thousands of dollars doing this, NO JOKE! I paid 1500 for a semester of community college and 5000 for a semester of a big name in-state university! Just make sure u speak with an adviser and see if all ur basic credits will be transferable to ur preferred college! Colleges are more willing to accept someone who has a high college GPA from a community college than someone with a high sat or act score and no college experience so it’s easier to get into tougher schools!
author: @mothercatt

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body: As someone that went to UCLA for a summer camp once, I like to say that the dorms are SO SMALL. I got lucky and got placed in a 2 people dorm, but most other people got placed in a 3 people dorm, but they are the same size. I saw their room when they opened the door and my god it was so crammed.
author: @asirfhdjzxcjv

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body: I went there and they dont care if you graduate or not.  you are an ant in an ocean.  "Open enrollment" still gives me trauma over 20 years later
author: @A22208

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body:
Just a reminder for anyone blinded by Ivy League schools:

Once you get your first job after graduation, where you got your degree doesn’t matter anymore. After that, future jobs mostly care about your previous job experience. Sure, you could argue a degree from these prestigious universities is more likely to get you a better job, but is it really worth the tax on your mental health for 4 years and the thousands of dollars in student loan debt you’ll be paying off for who knows how long? IMO it’s not

You can get a slightly less prestigious degree at a slightly less prestigious university and turn out just as fine if not better!!
author: @qqalaxyy

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body:
I’m the oldest, and UCLA was my dream school, but I thought my family couldn’t afford it so I went to state school and always felt bad, especially when my younger sister decided to go and got in and got to live my dream. It turns out I lived the dream however, as I have now graduated and live with her in apartments nearby campus and everyday I see her experience as a student here it makes me grateful I spent less money for a perfectly good education that paid more attention to who I was as a person than a big name school that only cares about those who have money.

Coming from someone whose family isn’t the richest, UCLA and big name schools only work if you have the money to make opportunities happen. Real people have to work and go to school which gives you no time for the benefits a big name school has to offer. I see my sister busting her ass every day, she’s the hardest working person I know and absolutely deserves to be here, yet I still feel as though she’s getting scammed because she doesn’t have time to take any opportunities the school and it’s name has to offer. Want to study abroad? That costs money. Want to live in a $3,700 a month apartment to escape campus housing? That costs money. Unless you have money, don’t go to a big name school I think you’ll just be a small fish in a big pond, no matter how talented you are.
author: @Scoobertdoobert169

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body:
Irvine graduate here, finished thirty years ago. What’s really pernicious about the whole public school acceptance process in California is that you need an obscene weighted GPA just to be considered for admission. And I don’t see any evidence that the caliber of student is significantly better than it was when I went to university.

So what’s changed? High school grade inflation? Schools not keeping up with state population growth? Under-reliance on SATs? (UC doesn’t consider them). Beats the * out of me.

All I know is that my kid is applying to Irvine in the fall, he’s way smarter and harder working than I was when I went, and his odds of getting in are way worse than mine were back then.

And for what? Four years of torture for a deflated grade? (btw I never saw this at Irvine but undergrad friends who went to UCLA did complain about grade curve deflation back then too.)
author: @michaelz6555

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body: Suddenly... my local 4-year public college experience feels like a walk in the park!! Heck, even my first semester of grad school is nothing like this! 😮

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body: As a California resident, the BEST thing you can do is go to a community college or 2 or 3 years then transfer to a CSU. I absolutely ADORE my csu (East Bay) and my community college experience was perfect too
author: @chelsey8737

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body: For anyone reading this it’s fine getting rejected it’s not the end of the world. What I did is go to a community college and got my associates than when it came to my bachelors you just go to a slightly better college or just stay at the community college. It’s literally the same education WAY cheaper and way more chill. It’s also the time where you try to figure yourself out there’s no point wasting THOUSANDS just so you can drop out. Take things slow and take baby steps you’ll be fine.
author: @IDK-gd5po

title: The CONS of going to UCLA for college
body: I go to a small private university (only bc they gave me more scholarships than public university’s in my state, otherwise I wouldn’t be able to afford this blessed education) and this is exactly how it is at mine too. Signing up for classes is so cut throat that we all stay up til midnight the night that registration opens, before the clock strikes we already have our curseres hovering over check out, and we always crash the website bc we all hit submit at the same time. Yet people still can’t get into the classes they need bc seniors get first dibs, then junior and so on. College just be too competitive for no reason!!!! Dorms are also tiny and not taken care of or maintained by the school, and we only have one cafeteria, a Starbucks, and a chick-fil-a that all either take forever to get your stuff and or give you food poisoning. Those of you who don’t get accepted to college/university take it as an omen!
author: @AbigailWatson-d5i

body: UC Berkeley is apparently the only UC not on quarter system - we're on semester system (from a currently uc berkeley student)

title: Day in the Life: Heart Surgeon
body: Thank you all for the views and kind comments. We don't have anything to promote but would love a THANK YOU note/photo/video to share with our caregivers to give them strength as they battle COVID-19 from the frontlines. Please reply here or mention us on Twitter or Instagram (@AdventistHC) with your message of thanks to our nurses, doctors and team. #HeroesWorkHere

title: Day in the Life: Heart Surgeon
body: When he said "the important thing is I'm not nervous" I instantly felt much calmer and I don't even have to undergo heart surgery

title: Day in the Life: Heart Surgeon
body:
Being a cardiac surgeon is very tough,
I went through 2 years of vigorous training to become a cardiovascular surgeon but later i changed my speciality since i was not feeling very satisfied with these extreme long hours of operation and procesures.
Hats off to this brilliant soul, may god bless him.
author: @doctordoctor3266

title: Day in the Life: Heart Surgeon
body: Fun fact: this man saved my fathers life. I will never forget the hours, minutes, seconds I sat waiting with my mom outside of surgery doors. For him to calmly come out, put his hand on my shoulder, and told me everything was going to be alright. Truly the nicest, most humble doctor I have ever met. If you ever read this sir, thank you. - Mark
author: @CoachClassic

title: Day in the Life: Heart Surgeon
body: As a paramedic, what he says at 2:35 is so true. Its really only "scary" when you have tried what you know should work/ MUST work, but it dosnt. An intubation for example, for your first time is definitely nerve wracking for obvious reasons, but after the first time its not scary until you nailed the whole process only to find its still not working, then you really have to think haha.

title: day in a life of an Indian student in USA 🇺🇸
body: I’m Indian and it makes me feel disappointed in our Indian people because when they see someone doing better than them in life, they get jealous, envious and negative. They start making fun of that person. Gargipawar, you’re a good person. Don’t take these losers seriously. They are upset that you are in USA and they don’t have the same opportunity as you do. Ignore them! Do well in life regardless of these negative people and their hate and you keep progressing forward in all areas of your life. Make your parents proud. Good luck! ❤
author: @igo7434

title: A Day in the Life of an Architecture Major
body: At my school 30% of the new architecture students dropped out after the first midterm of their freshman year, and I’ve heard that on the wall of the studio they have a “memorial” of names of the people that dropped out, as well as the date
author: @dapz

title: A Day in the Life of an Architecture Major
body: My dad is an architect. Been working as one for a VERY long time. Every once and a while we pass things and he can say “hey, I helped model that”. It’s cute.
author: @Roinkki

title: A Day in the Life of an Architecture Major
body: i studied architecture. our first year second semester was the semester they called the “purge semester” aka where they made everything so hard they weeded out the students who weren’t right for architecture.
author: @nailsbyannagrey

title: A Day in the Life of an Architecture Major
body: I’m a designer, architect for division 27 ( RCDD ) and work with 24 different architects and engineers. It’s called redlining and it’s a part of the process. You complete a draft and print it to bluebeam where your team corrects mistakes and sends it back to be corrected. It’s a team effort and that’s exactly how it works. I’ve been a designer for ten years now and I absolutely love everything about it. We are currently designing upgrades to the VA hospitals and the EHR systems for the doctors and staff. Very proud to be helping the Vets.
author: @NoMoreBigRatRace

title: A Day in the Life of an Architecture Major
body: my dads was an architect, he was one for about 30 years before the stress got to him, he began drinking and now hes a sack on the couch. he was a fun guy tho definitely pursue this

title: A Day in the Life of an Architecture Major
body: No matter how hard it will be and how much time i will waste, i still wanna do it. I couldn't see myself doing anything else. The only fear I have is that they will not give me enough freedom to designing. Nowadays architecture is very boring and ugly. I wanna change that but I don't know if people will let me.

title: Honest College Tour
body: I kid you not, I was on a tour of a Christian school, and one of the moms asks the tour guide/student: "Does the wifi block porn websites??"  in front of EVERYBODY. I felt so bad for the kid and we all just stood there in awkward silence 😭😭😭

title: Honest College Tour
body: Missing: The oh so funny "Don't walk on this somewhat decorative piece of sidewalk/threshold ."If a student does they will not graduate. If a prospective student does they will not get accepted. And, wait for it... If a parent does they will not get financial aid for their child. (polite laughter) . Also missing the famous story about how a student once called public safety for a ride late at night back to their dorm, and would you believe it, they actually got the ride. This was not the same student who called from the blue light safety phone because they saw a deer. True story!

title: If Berkeley were honest #berkeley #college #collegeadmissions
body: Televised college football games include brief commercials from the competing schools extolling their virtues. A recent UCB video, after images of the lovely campus and bright eyed students, ended with the declaration that the university was "of the people, by the people, and for the people" of California. That reminded me that the school's spirit and purpose have always been to educate, stimulate and encourage young people who reflect the remarkable diversity of the state. That fundamental tenet separates UCB from otherwise excellent private schools. It is truly remarkable public university.
author: @18bhfbpt61

title: If Berkeley were honest #berkeley #college #collegeadmissions
body:
Berkeley student here. Stanford does not live rent free in my head, and I'm not a CS student! 😂

Berkeley has a great program for Rhetoric and Film & Media Studies. The former is rarely offered at the same depth anywhere else in the country. The latter, while significantly less focused on production than UCLA and USC to the south, is probably one of the best media theory educations you can get anywhere.
author: @MagicalMedic

title: If Berkeley were honest #berkeley #college #collegeadmissions

body:
Speak for yourself. UC Berkeley is freaking awesome! Berkeley Law is top tier with best IP and Environment & Energy law courses in the world. Life Sciences and Physics Departments are pioneers. Haas is one the best business schools. Engineering is the most sought after. So many more amazing faculty and students at one of the best universities in the world.

Also we call Cal. Go Bears!
